narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your application turned away feels like a door slamming shut just as you're reaching for the handle. Our prediction model looked at your profile and saw a 76% chance of struggling to pass the bar exam down the line, painting a picture of a challenging path ahead based on patterns from thousands of students who've walked similar roads. What stood out most was your first-year decile of 1.0, the lowest rung in your school's ranking, signaling early academic hurdles that often snowball in law school demands. Right behind that was your third-year decile, also a 1.0, showing those struggles persisted rather than turning around as they do for many who succeed. Your LSAT score of 29 added to that concern, falling short of the benchmarks that typically light the way to bar passage for others. Even with your upper-middle family income, which the model saw as a modest push rather than a strong safety net, and your Asian background influencing the outlook toward tougher odds in this arena, the pieces didn't align favorably. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 and full-time student status nudged things further in that direction, while being female was the one gentle pull toward success in the model's view. It's like the model saw your story as one of consistent underperformance in competitive academic settings, despite your dedication to going full-time. This isn't about your worth or potential—it's a data-driven snapshot warning of risks ahead. Many students with early low deciles like yours have found ways to pivot, perhaps by targeting schools with stronger support or extra prep. You have the drive; now it's about finding the right bridge to cross that bar. Keep pushing—you've got this.
